From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Deputy Commissioner (Pakistan)](/w/index.php?title=Deputy_Commissioner_(Pakistan)&redirect=no "Deputy Commissioner (Pakistan)"))

Government position

**Deputy commissioner** (popularly abbreviated as "DC") is a chief administrative, land revenue officer/collector and representative of government in [district](/wiki/Districts_of_Pakistan "Districts of Pakistan") or an administrative sub-unit of a [division](/wiki/Divisions_of_Pakistan "Divisions of Pakistan") in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan").[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) The office-holder belongs to the commission of [Pakistan Administrative Service](/wiki/Pakistan_Administrative_Service "Pakistan Administrative Service")[[2]](#cite_note-2) erstwhile DMG/CSP or the Provincial Management Service erstwhile Provincial Civil Service.

The deputy commissioner is assisted by additional deputy commissioners (general, revenue, finance, and planning) and [assistant commissioners](/wiki/Assistant_commissioner_(police) "Assistant commissioner (police)") and district monitoring officer, deputy director development and General assistant revenue.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Divisional commissioner is assisted by additional commissioners (revenue, consolidation, coordination) and assistant commissioners (general, revenue) and director development.

In absence or transfer of the commissioner, deputy commissioner of division headquarter holds the acting charge, normally.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deputy_commissioner_(Pakistan)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: History")]

### Post devolution Local Government Reforms (2001 to 2008)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deputy_commissioner_(Pakistan)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Post devolution Local Government Reforms (2001 to 2008)")]

During the [presidency of Pervaz Musharraf](/wiki/Pervez_Musharraf#Presidency_(2001-2008) "Pervez Musharraf"), the office of deputy commissioner was replaced with [district coordination officer](/wiki/District_coordination_officer "District coordination officer"), except in Islamabad. Also, the office of [divisional commissioner](/wiki/Divisions_of_Pakistan "Divisions of Pakistan") was abolished. After his presidency, [provincial governments of Pakistan](/wiki/List_of_provincial_governments_of_Pakistan "List of provincial governments of Pakistan") again established this office through constitutional amendments.[[7]](#cite_note-:02-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

However the office of deputy commissioner is deprived of its previous powers of as a district magistrate. Subsequently, additional deputy commissioners and assistant commissioners does not execute the role of additional district magistrate and sub-divisional magistrate, respectively. Magisterial powers are now[*[as of?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] executed by judicial officers and judges.

### Post-independence of Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deputy_commissioner_(Pakistan)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Post-independence of Pakistan")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Deputy_Commissioner_of_Rawalpindi_District.jpg/250px-Deputy_Commissioner_of_Rawalpindi_District.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deputy_Commissioner_of_Rawalpindi_District.jpg)

Deputy commissioners/district magistrates of Rawalpindi District

The district continued to be the unit of administration after Indian partition and independence of Pakistan in 1947. Initially, the role of the district collector remained largely unchanged, except for the separation of most judicial powers to judicial officers of the district.

### Pre-independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deputy_commissioner_(Pakistan)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Pre-independence")]

District administration in [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") is a legacy of the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"). District collectors were members of the [British Indian Civil Service](/wiki/Indian_Civil_Service "Indian Civil Service") and were charged with supervising general administration in the district.[[10]](#cite_note-:2-10)

[Warren Hastings](/wiki/Warren_Hastings "Warren Hastings") introduced the office of the district collector in 1772. [Sir George Campbell](/wiki/George_Campbell_(1824%E2%80%931892) "George Campbell (1824–1892)"), [lieutenant-governor of Bengal](/wiki/List_of_governors_of_Bengal "List of governors of Bengal") from 1871 to 1874, intended "to render the heads of districts no longer the drudges of many departments and masters of none, but in fact the general controlling authority over all departments in each district."[[11]](#cite_note-:4-11)[[12]](#cite_note-:5-12)[[13]](#cite_note-:6-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Deputy_Commissioners_of_Attock_%28erstwhile_Campbellpur%29_district%2C_Punjab%2C_Pakistan.jpg/250px-Deputy_Commissioners_of_Attock_%28erstwhile_Campbellpur%29_district%2C_Punjab%2C_Pakistan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deputy_Commissioners_of_Attock_(erstwhile_Campbellpur)_district,_Punjab,_Pakistan.jpg)

Deputy Commissioners of **Attock** (erstwhile **Campbellpur**) district, Punjab, Pakistan

The office of a collector/DC during the [British rule in Indian subcontinent](/wiki/British_rule_in_India "British rule in India") held multiple responsibilities – as collector, he was the head of the revenue organization, charged with registration, alteration, and partition of holdings; the settlement of disputes; the management of indebted estates; loans to agriculturists, and famine relief. As district magistrate, he exercised general supervision over the inferior courts and in particular, directed the police work.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The office was meant to achieve the "peculiar purpose" of collecting revenue and of keeping the peace. The [superintendent of police](/wiki/Superintendent_of_police "Superintendent of police") (SP), inspector general of jails, the surgeon general, the divisional forest officer (DFO) and the chief engineer (CE) had to inform the collector of every activity in their departments.[[11]](#cite_note-:4-11)[[12]](#cite_note-:5-12)[[13]](#cite_note-:6-13)

Until the latter part of the nineteenth century, no native was eligible to become a district collector, but with the introduction of open competitive examinations for the British Indian Civil Service, the office was opened to natives. [Anandaram Baruah](/wiki/Anandaram_Baruah "Anandaram Baruah"), an eminent scholar of Sanskrit and the sixth [Indian](/wiki/India "India") and the first [Assamese](/wiki/Assamese_people "Assamese people") ICS officer, became the third Indian to be appointed a district magistrate, the first two being [Romesh Chandra Dutt](/wiki/R.C._Dutt "R.C. Dutt") and Sripad Babaji Thakur respectively.[[11]](#cite_note-:4-11)[[12]](#cite_note-:5-12)[[13]](#cite_note-:6-13)

## Responsibilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Deputy_commissioner_(Pakistan)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Responsibilities")]

The responsibilities of deputy commissioner vary from province to province. In Pakistan, these responsibilities changed with the passage of time.[[10]](#cite_note-:2-10) However, now the local government law of all provisional governments is similar to a large extent to the law of [Punjab Province](/wiki/Punjab,_Pakistan "Punjab, Pakistan"). Below some of the duties of a deputy commissioner are given:

* To supervise and monitor the discharge of duties by the [Assistant Commissioners](/wiki/Assistant_Commissioner "Assistant Commissioner") in the district.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)
* Coordination of work of all the sister offices and public facilities in the district.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)
* Efficient use of public resources for the integrated development and effective service delivery.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)
* To supervise and coordinate the implementation of the [government policies](/wiki/Government_policies "Government policies"), instructions and guidelines of the Government.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)
* To support and facilitate the offices and public facilities in the district.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)
* May convene a meeting for purposes of maintaining public order and public safety and safeguarding public or private properties in the District; and, the decisions taken in the meeting shall be executed by all concerned accordingly.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)
* Deputy commissioner is able to hold court sessions in criminal cases as justice of the peace, and monitors the performance of the assistant commissioner within the district.[[7]](#cite_note-:02-7)
